He plans to continue leveraging international institutions and community efforts to drive long-term change in the region. Rolando is from Honduras.

Qinhong (Roxana) Wang graduated from University of California, Berkeley as the top student in Comparative Literature and Classics. She published on cultural property law and the narratives surrounding cultural heritage and worked at an NGO combatting cultural racketeering. She has worked with UPenn to advise California tribes on the management of their cultural and natural resources and co-authored a national-level tourism management plan for the Sundarbans, Bangladesh’s World Heritage Site. She initiated and led projects with ICOMOS IICC and a Central American heritage education network. She aspires to become a leading voice at the intersection of cultural heritage and international relations. Roxana is from Nanjing, China.

Roxanne Stevenson-Brown is a global healthcare leader and Founder of Find Care Compare, a start-up that combats health system information inequality. Since graduating from the NHS Management Training Scheme, she has held numerous senior operational roles in technology, cancer, and neurosciences. She advocates for global healthcare collaboration as a China-United States Exchange Foundation scholar and Director of Young China Watchers. Academically, Roxanne completed the Elizabeth Garrett Anderson Healthcare Leadership programme, and holds an MSc China & Globalisation and BSc Political Economy. Through Schwarzman Scholars, Roxanne hopes to explore global business and policy interaction in health. Roxanne is from the United Kingdom.

Urunov Rozmet is a globally connected leader with significant experience in international business, fluent in six languages. He holds a BA in Chinese Language from Central China Normal University and an MA in Psychology from Hangzhou Normal University. He has made substantial contributions to Turkmenistan's economy in the agricultural sector and is an advocate for the Belt and Road Initiative. He has collaborated on various projects with the Turkmen Parliament and established international partnerships across China, the United States, Dubai, and Uzbekistan. As a co-founder of Golden Drop, he has demonstrated his entrepreneurial spirit. Urunov is from Turkmenistan.

Sage Lattman is a senior at Harvard University studying History. On campus, she serves as an editor and writer for The Harvard Crimson, specializing in investigative reporting. Beyond Harvard, she interned at a women’s rights nonprofit in Santiago, Chile, and at CAA in the company’s book publishing division. As a Schwarzman Scholar, she aims to deepen her understanding of China and U.S.-China relations. Sage plans to pursue a career in the media industry, fostering civil discourse and promoting new ideas in the public sphere. Sage is from the United States.

Sanjeev Musuvathy is an associate at the Boston Consulting Group, specializing in software private equity, digital transformations, and GenAI. A former nationally-recognized policy debater, he aspires to invest in technology entrepreneurs in emerging markets to drive growth. He previously designed a retail cryptocurrency trading product for a Tiger Global-backed Nigerian startup and defined go-to-market strategy for a Microsoft-backed Ethiopian HealthTech startup. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Sanjeev will leverage his experience building UNC’s first student-run venture capital fund and insights from China’s Digital Silk Road to address the late-stage startup investment gap in emerging markets. Sanjeev is from the United States.

Sasha Ofori, a BSc Computer Science graduate from Ashesi University, is a design research lead tackling complex issues in education and health across Africa. As a primary caregiver for a neurodivergent family member, she directs the Inclusive Employment Program at Multikids Inclusive School, creating job opportunities for neurodiverse young adults. Sasha also founded the Abakan-Dikan Fellowship, which supports young caregivers with scholarships, family counselling, therapy, and career guidance to pursue their education and dreams while managing their caregiving responsibilities. As a Schwarzman Scholar, she aims to enhance her expertise in China's governance, sustainability, and innovation to drive impactful solutions. Sasha is from Ghana.

Sharon Li is a business manager on Capital One’s Social Impact Advisory team, driving private sector innovation for socioeconomic mobility. While studying Global Affairs at Yale, she researched technology’s impact on Chinese and American societies and was a two-time Light Fellow in Beijing. Sharon also founded and leads a nonprofit focused on digital access and literacy. As a Schwarzman Scholar, she aims to connect socially driven business innovations across sectors and borders while deepening her understanding of China’s policies supporting marginalized communities. Sharon is from the United States.

Kaiwa (Shaun) Ho is a Hong Kong-based senior analyst at Wallbrook, a U.K.-based ESG corporate intelligence consultancy. He holds a bachelor’s degree in Foreign Service from Georgetown University and a master’s in European and International Public Policy from the London School of Economics. During his undergraduate studies, he also studied abroad at the Hebrew University in Jerusalem and University College London. Shaun founded his school’s first environmental club and served on the leadership boards of the Jewish Students Association and the European Club. As a Schwarzman Scholar, he aims to advance cooperation between China and the E.U. on environmental and climate protection. Shaun is from Macau S.A.R., China.

Shiqian Cui graduated from Tufts University with a double major in International Relations and Child Study & Human Development, focusing on globalization and transnational identities. With diverse experience in both the energy and education sectors, Shiqian has worked closely with the New Energy Vehicle (NEV) industry and taken on leadership roles in NGOs such as Strong Women, Strong Girls and the United Nations Association of Greater Boston. Her work in education emphasizes gender equity, youth mentorship, and the UN Sustainable Development Goals. She aims to build public-private partnerships that drive sustainable development and foster collaboration between China and the global community. Shiqian is from China.

Siqiao Liang studies Social Research and Public Policy at New York University Abu Dhabi and interns at the university’s Institute for Peace and Conflict Resolution. He has worked with refugees and immigrants in Jordan, Turkey, Lebanon, Syria, Argentina, Bolivia, Uruguay, and the UAE. In Amman, he conducted research at the Jordanian Higher Population Council and coordinated education programs with the Jesuit Refugee Service. He is currently exploring China’s role in global refugee governance. Siqiao serves as the student liaison between New York University Abu Dhabi and the Chinese Embassy and is vice president of the Chinese Students and Scholars Association. Siqiao is from China.

Stephanie Lin studies Applied Mathematics in Economics at Harvard. She served on the executive team of the Institute of Politics, the largest club on campus, where she hosted economic policymakers and spearheaded the Institute of Politics One Fund. She also served as chief of staff for the Harvard Economics Review and was part of the founding cohort of the Harvard Economics Labs. Stephanie interned at the Department of Commerce and worked as a proprietary trader on the Macro/Econ desks at D.E. Shaw and PIMCO. Her efforts focus on shaping a U.S. industrial policy with American characteristics. Stephanie is from the United States.

<p style="text-align: left;">Chuanyuan (Suzanne) Liu graduated from the University of Pennsylvania's Wharton School with concentrations in Finance and Business Analytics, along with minors in International Relations and Comparative Literature. Passionate about the fashion industry, she has interned at various consumer VC firms and Lanvin Group. Currently, as an associate at Alvarez & Marsal’s Consumer & Retail Group, Suzanne focuses on optimizing operations and strategies for global apparel companies. As a Schwarzman Scholar, she aims to foster a fashion ecosystem that promotes Chinese cultural traditions and environmental sustainability. She also facilitated cross-border dialogue through the Wharton Asia Exchange Journal and the Penn Wharton China Summit. Suzanne is from Beijing, China.</p>

Tania Martinez’s passion lies in empowering immigrant and underserved communities. At the NYC Department of Small Business Services, Tania has led the agency in multi-faceted manners, including co-founding New York City’s inaugural Latino American Small Business Task Force and leading efforts to connect newly-work authorized asylum seekers to employment. She holds a degree in Political Science, Philosophy, and Global Studies from Rutgers University - Camden. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Tania hopes to develop insight into how local government can better unlock immigrants' economic potential through exposure to China’s innovative state-led policy implementation as a model. Tania is from the United States.

Tianxin Li graduated from the University of California, San Diego with a degree in Statistics and pursued further studies in Comparative and International Education at Columbia University. She is passionate about using sports as a global language for cultural exchange between China and the international community. During the Winter Olympics, Tianxin served as a national technical official, ensuring seamless event operations through international collaboration. She also led a team of 30 to produce a figure skating reality show for CCTV featuring over 100 participants. Tianxin is determined to advance sports diplomacy through her work and future aspirations. Tianxin is from China.

Ting Kat Tony Luo is a sustainability strategist, urban planner and policy manager. Tony holds a Bachelor of Environments from the University of Melbourne. Whilst at University, Tony was the executive of AIESEC, a global youth organisation championing cross-cultural competencies. After graduation, Tony begun work in government delivering Australia’s largest infrastructure and transport projects. Tony hopes to collaborate with international communities to accelerate the global transition to a circular economy by creating eco-friendly precincts. Tony became the co-president of the United Nations Association of Australia’s Young Professional Network. Tony also spearheaded thought leadership on China’s smart cities thinking. Tony is from Australia.

Tra Nguyen graduated from Szent Istvan University with a bachelor’s degree in Wildlife Management Engineering. He currently works for the Young Southeast Asian Leaders Initiative - Mekong program at the Education Development Center. He also engages thousands of youth in policy advocacy as the founder of Vietnam Youth Parliament - Mekong Program, leader of Young Key Author Group on Just Energy Transition, and official representative of Vietnam at the Youth Sounding Board for E.U. in ASEAN. As a Schwarzman scholar, he hopes to learn from China’s experience to adapt local policies for climate change and sustainable development. Tra is from Vietnam.

Victoria Agostini holds a B.A. in International Relations from Pomona College. Following graduation, she joined Flexport, a technology-driven freight forwarder, as an operations associate. She later transferred to Denmark to support Flexport Europe’s commercial expansion. Now a senior account manager, she helps businesses navigate the complex logistics market and drive supply chain improvements. She also co-manages Affected, a music and art collective that produces experimental concerts in Copenhagen. Through Schwarzman Scholars, she will study China’s trade policy to develop international collaboration strategies for the freight industry to minimize disruptions during geopolitical crises. Tori is from the United States.

Vilas Annavarapu is a passionate educator, social entrepreneur, and environmental justice advocate. As the Environmental Justice Fellow for the Great Plains Institute, he works with communities to co-create Mississippi's first Just Transition plan through community engagement, research, and advocacy. Further, Vilas is the co-founder of the nonprofit Riverside Collective, a community cooperative in Jackson, Mississippi, that addresses issues of economic democracy, equitable education, and worker ownership. He holds a master’s in teaching from the University of Mississippi and is a Jefferson Scholar alumnus of the University of Virginia. Vilas is from the United States.

Jie (Vincent) Liu is a tech entrepreneur advocating for cross-cultural exchange through tea. A Duke University Computer Science graduate, he founded MyTeaPal in 2020, a community-focused mobile app and subscription service connecting over 20,000 tea enthusiasts worldwide and shipping curated teas to more than 50 countries. At Duke, he founded the CommuniTEA club, which continues fostering a strong community around tea culture. His work earned him selection for the Clinton Global Initiative University, the Melissa & Doug Entrepreneurs program, and the Watson Institute Flagship Fellowship. Vincent aims to promote tea appreciation, mindfulness, and global connections through technology. Vincent is from China.

Wafia Zia is currently a procedural advisor in the House of Commons. She is a procedural advisor to the Committee on Standards, the disciplinary body which oversees breaches of the Code of Conduct. As part of this role, Wafia has worked with Parliaments internationally to improve lobbying and transparency laws, most recently visiting Lesotho to draft a revised Code of Conduct. Wafia was named on the Forbes 30 under 30 list for Social Impact. She has previously worked on the Transport and Public Accounts Select Committees, advising on several high-profile inquiries. Wafia is from the United Kingdom.

Yanning Liu will graduate from the University of Manchester with a degree in archaeology and anthropology. She has participated in significant fieldwork at top archaeological and cultural institutions such as Beijing’s Palace Museum and the Yin Ruins in Anyang, Henan. She creatively planned and led community cross-cultural exchange activities, building a bridge for dialogue between Eastern and Western civilizations for residents. As a female youth representative, she spoke at the Belt and Road Women's Forum and Youth Leadership Forum, showcasing women's leadership in archaeology and global cultural exchange in the new era. Yanning is from China.

Yilin Chen is a consultant at Boston Consulting Group, specializing in the intersection of finance, climate, and social impact. She received her B.A. in Global Affairs and Economics alongside a French certificate from Yale University, where she led client acquisition for a social impact consulting organization and represented Yale in the National Fed Challenge. Through internships at StepStone Group and Caixin Global, she researched the business implications of China’s regulatory changes. She hopes to study China’s climate finance landscape and support capital flows for environmental and social impact. She plays the carillon, an instrument of bells. Yilin is from China.

Yu Ci Faye Ng is a doctor and aspiring clinician-innovator. She is a Clinical Fellow with the Duke-National University of Singapore Health Innovator Program, a regular columnist for the Straits Times, and a recipient of the Prime Minister’s Book Prize. She is the founder of Humans of Medicine YLL, a humanistic project sharing the stories of healthcare workers, and the Medical Adjacent podcast, which uncovers the intersection of medicine and adjacent fields like tech, innovation and the humanities. She previously undertook a gap year to explore medical entrepreneurship in Boston, and led various projects to improve healthcare access for marginalized communities. Faye is from Singapore.

Yutao Gong is a Leaders for Global Operations fellow at MIT, earning an MBA and a Master’s degree in Civil and Environmental Engineering with a focus on manufacturing and operations. Yutao has academic, work and social engagement experiences in China, the U.S., Jordan and Denmark. Yutao was previously a consultant at Boston Consulting Group working on manufacturing, agriculture, sustainability, and renewable energy-related projects, spending 2 years in Chicago and 1 year in Greater China as a Global Ambassador. Yutao graduated magna cum laude from Duke University with double majors in Environmental Science and Statistics, where she organized the Duke China-U.S. Summit. Yutao is from Shanghai, China.

Zachary Valentine is a senior at Brigham Young University double majoring in Neuroscience and Chinese. He is a neuroscience research leader at the Yorgason Lab, a founding member of the Rural Public Health Scholars program, and has worked extensively with refugee health initiatives. He has spent his past two summers conducting chronic pain research at Duke University and studying Mandarin at National Taiwan Normal University in Taipei. In the future, he aims to improve global healthcare systems and health policy, particularly focusing on underserved communities. Zach is from the United States.

Zilu Wang is passionate about the intersection of AI and art, with a particular focus on AI music. He has founded several AI-related projects, such as using large language models (LLMs) to help multinational companies with tariff code lookup, as well as using LLMs to advise college students on course selection. Zilu is dedicated to building great products, marketing them, and recruiting talented people to join his ventures. Prior to his work in AI, he was a full-time music producer, composing for various artists and events, including the Tsinghua Global Youth Dialogue summit at Tsinghua University. Zilu graduated from Cornell University. Zilu is from China.

Ajay Sawant is a veterinary doctor and conservation communicator. He currently serves as the media and brand engagement director at Bow Seat, a multi-award-winning nonprofit that empowers youth through scholarships and grants. He founded Generation Artivism, an initiative that empowers youth with creative advocacy for social impact. Ajay also leads as the president of ThinkOcean, a nonprofit active in over a dozen countries working on marine literacy, restoration, and policy. A fellow at The Asia Foundation, he’s a North American Association for Environmental Education 30 under 30 lister, Canadian High Commission’s 29 for 29 Changemaker, and the youngest strategic communications advisor for the UN Ocean Decade. Ajay is from India.

Justine (Akorfa) Dagadu is an innovator who believes trash talk can change the world. She graduated from MIT with a bachelor's degree in Chemical-Biological Engineering. She founded Ishara, a venture transforming recycling in Ghana by connecting informal waste-pickers to transparent, efficient systems with the potential to scale across growth markets. At MIT, she researched how enzyme-polymer systems can be designed to break down plastics at the end-of-life. This work has been recognized internationally through publications and awards, including the CellPress Rising Scientist Award. She aspires to establish a materials innovation hub in Africa, tackling the end-of-life of materials from plastics to e-waste. Akorfa is from Ghana.

Aleena Gul is originally from northwest Pakistan and grew up near Washington, D.C. Conflict, poverty, and geopolitical rivalries in her home region have shaped her focus in global affairs with a focus on international development, global migration, and the energy sector. At Yale, she co-founded the Ivy Future of Pakistan Conference, the largest student-led Pakistan conference in North America. She also leads the Salus Populi Foundation, the student affiliate of Yale’s Economic Growth Center, as well as the Refugee Immigrant Student Education. She aims to pursue a career in U.S. foreign policy and international relations. Aleena is from the United States.

Aleksandr (Alek) Kuzmenchuk graduated Phi Beta Kappa from the College of William and Mary in 2024, where he studied international relations and data science. For a year, he researched heritage conservation as foreign policy as a Fulbright-Nehru student researcher in New Delhi. He has held internships at the U.S. Senate Foreign Relations Committee, the Department of State, and a Department of Defense program and served as editor-in-chief of his university’s journal of international studies. He ended his gymnastics career as captain of his Division I team. He hopes to pursue a career in U.S. politics. Alek is from the United States.

Alex Tseng is a senior at Princeton University studying Electrical and Computer Engineering. Driven by a mission for affordable, reliable, and decarbonized electricity, he has published research on machine learning and optimization for improved grid control. The founder and president of WattOur, an early-stage company providing intelligent grid services, he works to bridge technical innovation with the social, regulatory, and geopolitical realities of power markets. Outside of energy, he is an awarded pianist and sits on the board of the Princeton University Energy Association, Orange Key Tour Guides organization, Club Tennis Team, and Woodcarving Club. Alex is from the United States.

Alexander (Alex) Cheng is a rising leader in Australia’s social sector. At Social Ventures Australia, he advises leading nonprofits, philanthropies, and governments on strategies to alleviate disadvantage. He began his career at L.E.K. Consulting, where he co-developed the inaugural First Nations Strategy for Australia’s national multicultural broadcaster. Passionate about inclusive public systems, Alex now focuses on how technological innovation in service delivery can rebuild trust and improve outcomes for marginalized communities. He was named a 40 Under 40 Most Influential Asian-Australian for advancing culturally diverse leadership and spoke at the World Economic Forum’s Annual Meeting in Davos. Alex is from Australia.

Weei (Alicia) Tien is an investment banking professional and climate strategist focused on ASEAN–global cooperation. She began her career in Southeast Asia and China, working on cross-border IPOs and M&A projects while advising companies on global expansion. She has organized large-scale forums and contributed to multimillion-dollar deals. Alicia founded the Tsinghua China-ASEAN Association to strengthen regional academic and policy ties, and serves as Malaysia’s representative to the EU in ASEAN Youth Sounding Board. A One Young World Net Zero Scholar and Global Citizen Youth Leader, she began her master's in Management at Tsinghua University at age 19. Alicia is from Malaysia.

Alison Lee graduated from Princeton University, majoring in Computer Science with minors in Entrepreneurship and Tech and Society. Her mission is to empower and connect female entrepreneurs around the world. She founded FemaleFounded, an undergraduate conference created to advance this goal. Alison also strengthened international dialogue by leading Business Today, an organization that connects undergraduate students globally with influential business leaders. She is eager to learn about the entrepreneurial ecosystem in China and at Tsinghua University. Currently, as an associate at Boston Consulting Group, Alison has explored the technology and private equity sectors. Alison is from the United States.

Amanda Gonzalez Mejia studies political economy at Duke Kunshan University. As a freshman, she founded the DKU Pre-Law Society, building mentorships, internships, and a Sino-international moot court. She launched the Duke Impact Investing Group, training student analysts to deliver ESG consulting projects. As an NRDC Fellow, she created a Climate Resilience Funding Toolkit mapping over 50 sources and improving access for 21 climate adaptation and infrastructure projects. Her research models EU CBAM liabilities for Latin American exporters and tests China rerouting scenarios. She aims to design sustainable finance tools linking China and the Global South to fund green development. Amanda is from Venezuela and Spain.

Jiajie (Angel) Zhang is a senior at Harvard University studying Integrative Biology and Economics. Passionate about human–nature coexistence, she worked at The Nature Conservancy Shanghai to restore native biodiversity through urban habitats, the Mangrove Conservation Foundation to strengthen Greater Bay Area coastal resilience, and the Hong Kong Green Finance Association to advance capacity-building on nature-based solutions and biodiversity finance. Angel also conducts field research in Kenya with the Harvard University Davies Lab, using remote sensing to map human–lion conflict and inform mitigation strategies. As the co-president of the Harvard College China Forum, Angel is dedicated to fostering multilateral dialogue and collaborative solutions to global ecological challenges. Angel is from Hong Kong S.A.R., China.

Anita Bassey graduated from Northwestern University with a degree in Biomedical Engineering as a QuestBridge Scholar. She has held executive leadership roles for four years with the Northwestern National Society of Black Engineers, advancing mentorship and community initiatives in Evanston, IL. Anita has conducted research at the Feinberg School of Medicine, Abbott Laboratories, and the Rivnay Group, contributing to projects with clinical and technological impact. She also founded the NU Bike Club, a social venture promoting wellness, community-building, and collaboration. Inspired by her experiences in Nigeria and the United States, she seeks to expand global healthcare access through affordable diagnostic devices. Anita is from the United States and Nigeria.

Anna de Beer holds a first-class Bachelor of Science in Medicine and is completing her MBBS. She was appointed as a governor for Dorset HealthCare NHS Trust, orchestrating services for over 800,000 patients. Anna works as a senior editor for the British Student Doctor Journal and sits on Elsevier’s Student Board. An active researcher with multiple peer-reviewed publications on allergy and immunology, she has undertaken research in the U.K., Singapore, South Korea, and the U.S. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Anna aims to further foster U.K.-China collaboration in healthcare innovation to improve patient outcomes globally. Anna is from the United Kingdom and South Africa.

Arón Olegnowicz is a special education teacher serving 22 students with disabilities in a high-poverty elementary school through Teach For America. A two-time FLAS Fellow at Ohio State University, Arón lobbied on Capitol Hill to successfully secure over $85 million in continued government funding for study abroad scholarships. He has also channeled his passion for education into projects with the United Nations, U.S. Department of Defense, and Accenture. Inspired by China’s socioeconomic development model, Arón aims to expand access to quality education globally by integrating AI technology, public policy, and business. Arón is from the United States and Mexico.

KeiSam (Ashley) Choi is a passionate advocate for the healing power of sports. With a multicultural background spanning Shanghai, Macau, and New York and over a decade of immersion in athletics and business, she has evolved from a student-athlete to an emerging female leader in the sports industry. Ashley holds a B.S. in Sports Management from New York University, during which she led youth development communication for the Jr. NBA basketball program in China. She now focuses on the art of storytelling in sports, leveraging its universal appeal to foster virtue and community well-being in a post-pandemic world. Ashley is from Shanghai and Macau S.A.R., China.

Ava Noelle Johnson is pursuing Economics at Spelman College, where she has combined her interests in public policy, business, and video game development to advocate for more equitable media representation. As an undergraduate, she has served as an Electronic Arts producer intern, a fellow at the JPMorganChase-backed Sadie Summit, a U.S. Bank Wall Street Fellow, and the vice president of her HBCU’s Chinese Culture Club. Ava has a passion for social justice, which she has conveyed as a Georgia Women’s Policy Institute alumna, where she spent hours advocating for legislation that would assist in ending the school-to-prison pipeline. Ava Noelle is from the United States.

Ayotomiwa Elesho is a first-class political science graduate of the University of Ibadan, Nigeria, and a 2024 Diana Award recipient. He founded Homeless Not Hopeless Nigeria, a youth-led organization that has raised homelessness awareness among over 10,000 Nigerians. He has impacted over 900 students through substance abuse campaigns and reached 150 elderly citizens through mental health advocacy. Selected as a 2021 Doha Debates Ambassador, Ayotomiwa joined 36 other changemakers from 17 countries to address global issues. He aspires to use social and entrepreneurial policies to improve the lives of homeless populations in Nigeria and Africa. Ayotomiwa is from Nigeria.

Barna Peterfi is a published author who has worked and studied across five countries, holding research and consulting roles, and is an incoming Andrássy National Security Fellow specializing in Eurasian geopolitics. At university, he built a youth foundation with over 150 members, democratizing entry points into policy debates for students. He is passionate about the intersection of energy policy, sustainability, and geopolitics, an emerging field he terms geosustainability. Through Schwarzman Scholars, he seeks to leverage Chinese cultural engagement and green tech leadership to forge a Sino-European consensus on energy connectivity. He graduated with distinction from the University of Edinburgh. Barna is from Hungary.

Benedict Ballman is a Cornelius Vanderbilt Scholar of Public Policy from Vanderbilt University. He is the founder of Tomorrow in Focus, a nonprofit driving AI policy development and integration at intergovernmental institutions such as UNCCD, UNESCO, and UNICEF, as well as forums such as UNCCD COP16 and Dubai Future Forum. As a pipeline engineer at Inkwell AI, he developed pro-IP AI models and tools for creativity. During Schwarzman Scholars, Benedict will study China’s interim AI frameworks to develop AI-IP policies that support creatives such as himself. Benedict is an award-winning filmmaker and avid outdoorsman. Benedict is from the United States.

Bryanna Entwistle graduated from Dartmouth College in 2023 with honors in History and minors in Government and Asian Studies. At the Asia Society Policy Institute, she researches Southeast Asia and U.S. policy in the Indo-Pacific. Her analysis has been published in several major news outlets, and she has been recognized as a rising leader in foreign policy by the Asia Foundation and the Asia Pacific Foundation of Canada. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Bryanna hopes to study China’s strategy in Southeast Asia with the goal of improving the future of American diplomacy in the region. Bryanna is from the United States and Canada.

Camalah Saleh is a first-generation Yemeni immigrant and graduate of California State University, Fresno, where she earned a dual degree in Political Science and Communication. She attended Fresno State on a full-ride scholarship as a Smittcamp Family Honors College Scholar. As student body president, she advocated for over 25,000 students and led projects centered on food insecurity and accessibility. She founded Students for Palestinian Liberation, growing the club to over 1,000 members in its first year. Camalah hopes to reform the U.S. immigration system to be just and economically sound. Camalah is from the United States.

Camden Dempsey is an entrepreneur and student-athlete from the University of Colorado, where he played Division I football and earned the title “The Governor” as the team's spokesperson under Coach Deion Sanders. Off the field, he co-chaired the Big 12 Student-Athlete Advisory Committee and is also the founder of VerEstate, an AI-powered title insurance startup that has raised six-figure funding. A Boettcher Scholar, Eagle Scout, and Coolidge Senator, Camden graduated with honors and distinction in Finance and Marketing and also holds a master’s degree in Real Estate. He aspires to bridge U.S.–China economic relations through entrepreneurship, real estate, and development finance. Camden is from the United States.

Catherine Zeng is a pianist who views music as a powerful tool for cultural diplomacy and global healing. Trained by Dan Zhaoyi and now studying at the Royal College of Music, she founded a student-led charity at 11, bringing music therapy to pediatric leukemia wards. Her solo recital raised 2.67 million yuan to fund scholarships for underprivileged Tibetan students. In 2022, she became the youngest Chinese NGO delegate to sign a memorandum of understanding with the World Health Organization. Through Schwarzman Scholars, she aims to leverage music, philanthropy, and policy to foster cross-cultural exchange and sustainable global leadership. Catherine is from Hong Kong S.A.R., China.

Chengcheng Song studies Economics and Statistics & Data Science at the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA). He recently worked at the United Nations Statistics Division, where he contributed to data collection, analysis, and reporting initiatives as part of a global assessment on sustaining statistical capacity in low- and middle-income countries. Passionate about the intersection of technology and policy, Chengcheng has also worked at the Albright Stonebridge Group in Washington, D.C. and ByteDance in Beijing, where he advised on AI regulations, export controls, and investment restrictions. Chengcheng is currently a co-founder of Gizmo Payments, a fintech startup based in Lusaka, Zambia, focused on expanding digital financial inclusion in emerging markets. Chengcheng is from China.

Chengxi Jiang explores the dialogue between industrial policy and emerging technologies. She is currently a Global Chemicals Equity Research Associate at Jefferies in New York, with prior experience at Goldman Sachs and Xinhua News Agency. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Chengxi is dedicated to driving innovation-led growth in traditional industries through research, capital coordination, and greater transparency. Originally from China’s industrial heartland, she is passionate about supporting underprivileged communities, having served in leadership roles with NGOs such as Learning Enterprises, the Red Cross, and A Better Community. Chengxi holds degrees from Nanjing University and Columbia University, where she focused on communications, international finance policy, and data analytics. Chengxi is from China.

Chinwendum (Blossom) Egbude is a lawyer and policy professional who earned an MPhil in Public Policy from the University of Cambridge as a Mastercard Foundation Scholar. She has served as a Youth Leader with the Center for International Private Enterprise and as a BeyGOOD Policy and Partnerships Fellow with Global Citizen. Recognized among Africa’s Top 50 Rising Motherland Moguls Under 30, she has received the Diana Award, the U.S. Consulate Nigeria-America Partnership Award, and was nominated for a Future Awards Africa Prize. She aspires to advance inclusive governance across Africa. Chinwendum is from Nigeria.

Christelle Barakat is a researcher at the Lebanese Center for Policy Studies and graduated from the University of North Carolina at Greensboro with an M.A. in Peace and Conflict Studies and a concentration on International Peace Development through the Fulbright program. She has led multiple global Model UN conferences, introducing youth to conflict resolution and diplomacy. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Christelle hopes to examine China's potential role in bridging sustainable development and nuclear disarmament and non-proliferation. She is a UN Youth Leader for a World Without Nuclear Weapons and a 2023 Diana Award recipient. Christelle is from Lebanon.

Daniel Martin will graduate from the University of Montana, where he studied History and Environmental Science as a Presidential Leadership Scholar. He has worked across local, state, and federal government, including internships with state legislators and U.S. Senator Jon Tester, and served as a Demmer Policy Scholar in Washington, D.C. He helped the City of Missoula develop a cost-effective climate strategy and led a research project in Singapore examining the country’s climate policies for potential application in Montana. Daniel also studied abroad in Ireland and China and is committed to addressing the climate crisis through public policy for rural communities across the region. Daniel is from the United States.

Daphne Chebesi graduated from the University of Edinburgh with an MSc in Africa and International Development. Passionate about youth development, she has coordinated initiatives across different nonprofits in Cameroon and Ghana to improve educational access and outcomes for young people from underserved communities. She also serves as a Senior Fellow and board member of the Melton Foundation, fostering cross-cultural collaboration and global citizenship for youth across five different continents. Daphne aspires to lead a nonprofit that creates safe and inclusive physical learning spaces where young people can access opportunities and develop their potential. Daphne is from Cameroon.

David Hischier graduated with a bachelor’s in Business Administration from the University of St. Gallen, where he was a scholar at the Center for Entrepreneurship. Besides his studies, he founded Luna Socks, a custom sock manufacturer with operations in Germany and China. Within three years, David grew the business into the leading custom sock provider in Europe. Luna Socks operates an in-house production facility and serves over 200,000 customers across 15 markets. As a Schwarzman Scholar, he aims to explore the export of fast-moving consumer goods from China and innovate products for global markets. David is from Switzerland and Germany.

Deborah (Debbie) Madueke is a finance professional at Google and a cofounder of Replast, a plastic recycling company in Nigeria. Her studies in Economics and International Affairs at Northeastern University, strengthened by a semester in Hong Kong, sparked her interest in Chinese–African relations. At Google, she oversees a multibillion-dollar machine portfolio while advancing collaboration and inclusive culture. Drawn to Schwarzman Scholars for its immersive engagement with China’s global leadership, she aims to work with African governments and international institutions on development strategies grounded in local context, digital infrastructure, and entrepreneurship. Debbie is from Nigeria and the United States.

Diana Godinho studies International Studies and Public Management at Stetson University on a full scholarship. She founded PROAME, Brazil’s leading NGO addressing hunger, and spent three years leading Vest News, an awarded education initiative. She has represented Brazil at multiple international and UN forums, most notably as an invited member of the official civil society delegation to the 69th Commission on the Status of Women, where she presented research focused on gender and education. Her experience spans working in Brazil’s National Congress, supervising sustainable projects in Costa Rica, and coordinating high-level diplomatic events in Washington, D.C. Diana is from Brazil.

Divya Ganesan is completing a master’s in Computer Science and a bachelor’s in Political Science at Stanford University. She served as Stanford’s Student Body Vice President, receiving the Lloyd Dinkelspiel Award for service, and started Stanford's first all-girls cybersecurity team, the nationally recognized #CyberSuperGirls. Her honors thesis examined why AI companies join voluntary safety commitments. Divya hopes to leverage secure technology to strengthen global institutions and improve national security decision-making, all while making space at the table for others. Divya is from the United States.

Donia Elmansy works at Microsoft at the intersection of technology and government, managing investments that drive AI and cloud computing adoption in government agencies. She graduated magna cum laude from Harvard University with a degree in Government and Near Eastern Civilizations. She served as president of the Harvard Program for International Education and Harvard Model United Nations Dubai conference. Since graduating, Donia has continued teaching in classrooms in India, Indonesia, Egypt, the UAE, and Japan. Raised between Connecticut and Egypt, she aspires to help emerging economies adopt new technologies by drawing on insights gained in China. Donia is from the United States.

Qingyan (Elaine) Zhang double majors in Math and Public Policy Studies with a minor in Statistics at Duke University. On campus, Elaine serves as the president of the Public Policy Majors’ Union and the Executive Print Editor of the Duke Undergraduate Law Review. Elaine is passionate about leveraging data-driven tools to address social and policy challenges, with a particular interest in international data governance. Through her internship at a global non-profit organization and participation in the international conference, Elaine contributed to the research and conversations in the field. In the future, Elaine hopes to advance ethical and responsible international data governance frameworks. Elaine is from China.

Eli Yoichi Leadham is a Truman Scholar studying Sociology with a concentration in Criminology and minoring in English and Japanese at Wake Forest University. A National Collegiate Student Debate Champion finalist, they have coached debaters in the U.S. and the Asia-Pacific region. Drawing on their experiences, Eli interned for the U.S. Attorney’s Office of Sexual Offense to support survivors of gender-based and sexual violence. On campus, they also advocate for the LGBTQ+ community, serving as the inaugural violence prevention assistant and co-director of Advocacy Leaders in Interpersonal Violence Education. Eli holds citizenships in the United States and Japan.

Elliot Smith is a senior at Brown University studying Computer Science and International and Public Affairs, where he was elected to Phi Beta Kappa as a junior. On campus, he serves as editor-in-chief of the Brown Political Review, a 200-member undergraduate publication. His thesis research examines how states adapt frameworks for AI and data regulation, with a focus on governance models in the Global South. As a Schwarzman Scholar, he will study China’s approach to technology governance with the goal of pursuing a career at the intersection of law and diplomacy. Elliot is from the United States and Canada.

Emily Deng is passionate about human connection and institutional design. At Stanford University, she launched a social events newsletter that reached over 8,000 readers, managed more than $220,000 in student life funding, and revived traditions dating back to 1939. As a mentee at the Hoover Institution, she gained firsthand exposure to national security, and at Bain and IBM, she applied her background in human-computer interaction to drive innovation. Emily holds a bachelor's in Symbolic Systems and an M.S. in Computer Science. Ultimately, she aims to bring together diverse perspectives to advance AI alignment. Emily is from the United States.

Ernie Vita is a consultant at the Boston Consulting Group working across diverse functions and industries, with a particular focus on strategy, risk management, and operations within the energy sector. Passionate about mentorship, he volunteers with multiple organizations, helping students and young professionals navigate career decisions. Ernie is a summa cum laude graduate of Texas A&M University, where he studied geology and economics while serving as the president of the Student Geosciences Council. As a Schwarzman Scholar, he aims to explore China’s energy system, including the country’s leading role in renewable energy and innovation. Ernie is from the United States.

Esen Alimzhan uulu is a purpose-driven young leader, fintech professional, and social entrepreneur. He founded the NGO, Insan Develop, to close educational gaps in his village, helping over 100 students gain acceptance to top Kyrgyz universities. As co-founder of the Kyrgyz Club in Hungary, he fostered cultural exchange and built a community of 600 Kyrgyzstanis. As a Stipendium Hungaricum Scholar, he graduated from Corvinus University of Budapest and joined Wise’s competitive rotational graduate program. He aspires to be a global development leader, reducing poverty and improving the living standards in Central Asia by leveraging China’s development experience. Esen is from Kyrgyzstan.

Ethan Sperla serves as an advisor at the U.S. Department of Commerce, where he advances U.S. national interests through strategic engagement with China. He also helped launch the American AI Exports Program. Rooted in Flint, Michigan, Ethan is committed to revitalizing economically dislocated communities, expanding opportunity in his hometown while driving economic policy from Washington. He previously directed research at the Hoover Institution and spearheaded its Economic Statecraft Program. Ethan holds degrees in Economics and Management Science & Engineering from Stanford University. He aspires to strengthen U.S. international leadership while advocating for underserved communities. Ethan is from the United States.

Evie Steele is a student and journalist at Georgetown University’s School of Foreign Service. In the classroom, Evie studies Asian History and Politics, focusing her research on the evolution of Chinese press freedom over time. In the newsroom, she leads Georgetown’s newspaper, The Hoya, as editor-in-chief, publishing award-winning coverage of student protests, admissions controversies, and immigration issues. An aspiring foreign correspondent, she has covered international business and U.S.–China relations for NBC News, CNN, and Voice of America. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Evie hopes to understand how American media can better represent China beyond buzzwords. Evie is from the United States and the United Kingdom.

Faisal Minawi, a Computer Science graduate from the American University of Beirut, is an emerging technologist leader. As a Millennium Fellowship campus director, he guided peers in developing UNSDG-aligned projects, fostering collaborative leadership. Faisal's AI research projects explore technology's role in addressing societal challenges. An Aspire Leaders Program and McKinsey Forward Program alumnus, he combines technical expertise with strategic thinking. Faisal aims to link technology with geopolitics, applying his perspective as a technologist with Middle Eastern insights to shape policy and foster innovation in international development. He aspires to drive cross-cultural understanding through technological solutions. Faisal is from Turkey and Palestine.

Francis Mok is an artist manager and a Beijing Film Academy Cinematography graduate with eight years of cross-Pacific experience in China and the U.S. He has managed a variety of superstars, including Lay Zhang (EXO), Awich, and MC Jin, guiding them into the Billboard 200’s top 25 and helping them sell millions of albums. He has collaborated with Grammy-winning producers, secured placements on major Chinese shows, such as Rap of China, and brokered collaborations with icons such as Steve Aoki and Alan Walker. From curating cross-continental tours and festivals to shaping artist branding and market strategies, he brings a uniquely global perspective. Francis is from the United States.

Garrett Audet is a first-generation graduate with a dual degree in Business and Computing from Queen’s University. He has served in Canada’s military intelligence unit, represented the country at the World Trade Organization, and helped scale Neo Financial, the nation’s fastest-growing startup. Passionate about the intersection of government and innovation, Garrett has worked across the private, public, and nonprofit sectors, exploring how technology can build more responsive systems. As a Schwarzman Scholar, he aims to learn how such innovations can be better integrated into public institutions, helping governments meet the emerging challenges of the 21st century. Garrett is from Canada.

Gastón Ocampo is the secretary general of IPDAL in Lisbon, where he advances South-South cooperation through development projects benefiting thousands across Latin America and Africa. Previously at the Inter-American Dialogue in Washington, D.C., he shaped Latin America’s first subnational cooperation agenda with governments, international organizations, and multilateral development banks. He graduated with honors from Roanoke College, earning a double major in International Relations and Business Administration. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Gastón intends to explore China’s blended financing models to reinforce South-South cooperation as a viable alternative to traditional aid. He brings a global outlook shaped by diverse regional experiences. Gastón is from Argentina.

Gaurav Jung Pande co-founded PlantSat, Nepal’s leading agri-insurtech company that has protected over 6,700 farmers by leveraging AI and satellite technology. He studied Economics and Finance at New York University in Abu Dhabi. Gaurav's cross-disciplinary pursuits span financial analysis in Uganda’s renewable energy sector as a Princeton in Africa Fellow, leadership with Fortune 500 executives at The Adecco Group as the regional "CEO for One Month" winner, and product management at noon, MENA’s largest homegrown e-commerce platform. Gaurav aims to build enterprises at the intersection of technology, business, and cross-border capital flows to create equitable opportunities in emerging economies. Gaurav is from Nepal.

Giancarlo Ceja is an environmental policymaker whose work and passion lie in environmental justice, previously serving at institutions like the White House and the Energy and Commerce Committee. During his career in public service, he has implemented initiatives from the Inflation Reduction Act, the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, and Biden-Harris’ Justice40. As a Schwarzman Scholar and onward, Giancarlo strives to bridge the equity gaps in environmental policy and protect the marginalized communities most susceptible to the climate crisis. He holds B.A. degrees in Environmental Studies and International Relations from the University of Southern California. Giancarlo is from the United States.

Gleb Lisichenko is a bridge-builder between the East and West, shaped by experiences across the U.K., France, and China. His leadership spans transforming international payment operations, growing transaction flows from zero to more than $100 million within months, and managing relations with over 20 trading companies across APAC and EMEA. He has also organized cultural preservation expeditions conserving historic wooden architecture. Fluent in five languages and a graduate of the Moscow State Institute of International Relations and the University of International Business and Economics, he combines geopolitical risk analysis with business development expertise. Gleb is from Russia.

Of Indigenous Aymara roots, Gustavo Blanco is a socio-environmentalist committed to placing Indigenous knowledge at the forefront of conservation and restoration efforts. A sociology graduate from Princeton University, Gustavo’s research and service work as a Mellon Mays Fellow and co-founder of the Uru-Uru Team demonstrate how Indigenous, community-based initiatives can provide efficient solutions to global challenges like climate change. He served as the Class of 2025 senator and co-president of Natives at Princeton. As a Schwarzman Scholar, he aims to investigate the socio-environmental impact of China’s Belt and Road Initiative in South America, focusing on lithium exploitation. Gustavo is from Bolivia.

Hamza Khawaja is a law graduate of the Lahore University of Management Sciences. He led a national movement against the 31-year ban on student unions, prompting reforms for nearly two million students. As a partner at Taameer-AI, he oversees development of an AI transcription tool designed to reduce Pakistan’s judicial backlog by up to 50%. His politico-legal analyses through television, social media, and publications have earned more than one million views. Hamza worked at the World Bank and founded Pakistan’s first student-run policy think tank, consulting for the government. Hamza is from Pakistan.

Haoshan Hong is an entrepreneur and educator on a mission to reshape how people connect through language. Through internships at BCG and Meituan and having learned English, French, Turkish, and Korean, he combines a builder’s mindset with a strong business sense and hands-on experience across language, technology, and education. He built Fluentide, an AI-powered platform that reimagines the language learning experience, and founded a school helping both children and adults master English in more effective, human-centered ways. He is focused on applying AI to make high-quality language education more accessible and hopes to collaborate with leaders across academia, business, and public policy to make education more inclusive and transformative. Haoshan is from China.

Hpone Thit Htoo is a senior at Brown University studying Economics and International and Public Affairs. Passionate about foreign policy and geoeconomics, he has previously interned at CSIS and Deloitte and currently serves as Student Chair for Brown’s Economics Department. He co-founded BatAware, a grant-winning public health platform, heads advisory projects for international nonprofits, and curates panels for the Brown China Summit. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Hpone aims to examine how multilateral organizations can bridge divides and navigate fragmentation in a multipolar world, with a particular interest in China-ASEAN relations. Hpone is from Myanmar.

Husnaa Zamarai has held strategic roles at the Boston Consulting Group, Royal Bank of Canada, Toronto Metropolitan University, and a tech startup incubator supporting marginalized entrepreneurs. She currently works with NGOs in Morocco, designing income-generating projects and skills training programs for widows and at-risk youth. As the president of Canada’s largest student-elected business association, Husnaa led national award-winning initiatives addressing challenges like food insecurity, access to professional attire, student space revitalizations, and subsidizing course materials. She also created entrepreneurship and financial literacy programs benefiting over 8,000 newcomer women in Toronto. Husnaa is from Canada and Afghanistan.

Ilia Kosar founded and leads the digital transformation office at Epic Group, a global apparel manufacturer. He began his career as an executive trainee at Jardine Matheson before co-founding ventures in fintech and tech consulting in Singapore and Hong Kong, supported by partners such as Coinhako, Le Freeport, and Meinhardt. A math graduate of Yale-NUS College with interests in entrepreneurship and public policy, Ilia is passionate about bringing frontier technologies into traditional sectors, including manufacturing and construction. Ilia is from Russia.

Isaac McCurdy is a U.S. Army infantry officer. During his service, he has led mechanized, motorized, and dismounted infantry formations conducting missions as part of large-scale combat operations at the Army’s National Training Center in the Mojave Desert. At Cornell University, he majored in China and Asia Pacific Studies, played rugby, and performed stand-up comedy in Mandarin. At Schwarzman College, Isaac aims to develop solutions for multipolarity that he plans to refine and apply in a future career in diplomacy. He grew up with his family on the banks of the Potomac River in Virginia. Isaac is from the United States.

Ishita Kapoor is a technologist, entrepreneur, and public speaker who graduated with first-class honors in Computer Science. As Founder and CEO of eDrishti, she has developed solutions making STEM education accessible to over 202 million visually impaired students globally. Her work in inclusive education, assistive technology, and AI has earned recognition from Microsoft, Google, UNICEF, and the Indian government. She has secured over $12,000 in funding through partnerships with NGOs and tech organizations. Ishita has led over 35 teams to success and believes the Schwarzman Scholars will accelerate her vision. Ishita is from India.

Bayarjavkhlan (Jack) Bayanmunkh is a social entrepreneur and policy advocate dedicated to expanding global education access for Mongolian youth. He co-founded Gobi Scholars NGO and Academy, helping students earn scholarships to top universities and bridging Mongolian students with global education. During his undergraduate years, he led the 600-member Association of Mongolian Students in the United States. His policy work with Mongolia’s Parliament advanced higher education finance reform, and his research at the East–West Center focused on U.S.–Indo-Pacific relations. A Political Science and Economics major at Swarthmore College, Jack aims to establish a globally connected institute in Mongolia, where he was raised as a nomad. Jack is from Mongolia.

Jamaal (Jama) Willis graduated cum laude from Harvard College in Government and African and African American Studies. As the co-president of the Harvard Undergraduate Foreign Policy Initiative, he organized conferences in Turkey and South Korea while researching for the Pentagon, NATO, and foreign governments. At Harvard, Jama interned for the U.S. Department of State, the U.S. Embassy in Trinidad & Tobago, and Amnesty International. After Beijing, he aspires to be an international human rights attorney, advancing Caribbean socio-political rights for diaspora citizens and promoting regional institutional development. Jama is a dual citizen of the United States and Jamaica.

Xiaolong (James) Wang is pursuing a master’s degree in Foreign Service at Georgetown University, focusing on artificial intelligence and digital infrastructure regulation. He graduated from the University of Toronto with a double major in Political Science and Philosophy, where he led the Pre-Law Society, served on the University Tribunal adjudicating disciplinary cases, and directed Trinity College’s Academic Advising Program. He interned at the NATO Association of Canada, advancing public diplomacy initiatives. As Compliance Director at the G20 Research Group, he monitors member-state commitments and presented his team’s findings to world leaders at the 2025 G20 Summit in Johannesburg, South Africa. James grew up in Singapore and is from China.

Jasmine Zhao is the founder of the Refugee Parliament Conference, a pioneering platform that brings refugee voices into policymaking through partnerships with international organizations and policy leaders such as UNHCR and the Montreal Refugee Center. Jasmine has led NGOs supporting marginalized communities, launching educational programs and community drives that reached hundreds of underprivileged children across China. She has gained international professional experience through global corporations and conferences, including the World Peace Forum, where she coordinated over 100 international media outlets. A national golf athlete of China and accomplished musician who plays seven instruments, Jasmine pursued a double major in International Development Studies and Psychology at McGill University. Jasmine is from China.

Chit Hang (Jason) Chau is a journalist for Dow Jones and The Wall Street Journal, where he covered Asian markets, business, and economics. His writing has also appeared in The Economist and the Financial Times. He has interviewed leading policymakers and thinkers, covering major international events such as the Munich Security Conference. An MPhil graduate of the University of Oxford, he studied Social Policy and Intervention and served as editor-in-chief of The Oxford Student newspaper and editor of the Oxford Political Review. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Jason seeks to better understand China’s political economy and enhance international reporting on the country. Jason grew up between the U.S., the U.K., and China and is originally from Hong Kong.

Chin-Heng (Jason) Chiang graduated from the Chinese University of Hong Kong, majoring in Quantitative Finance and Risk Management Science and double minoring in Artificial Intelligence and Statistics. Passionate about finance, economics, and data science, he has interned at UBS Global Markets, CICC Research, Mastercard Data & Services, and Deutsche Bank Investment Banking. Harboring the motto of "always exploring," he was an exchange student at HEC Paris and is always ready for the next adventure. He is genuinely curiosity-driven and hopes to improve his understanding of the world through interdisciplinary perspectives. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Jason aspires to reduce the global imbalance in capital market development. Jason is from Taiwan.

Jira Trinetkamol is an urbanist, government affairs consultant, and aspiring policymaker dedicated to decentralizing Thailand’s development. To advance this mission, he advises a Member of Parliament on Thailand's national decentralization and reskilling policy. Jira worked at the California Governor’s Office, Brookings Institution, and U.S. Chamber of Commerce, co-organized a Thai–U.S. Trade & Investment Summit, and founded a nonprofit aiding Thailand’s homeless elderly. He is trilingual in Thai, English, and Mandarin and triple-majored in Economics, Political Science, and Urban Studies at UC Irvine. He aspires to study China's regional development strategies to strengthen Thailand's secondary cities. Jira is from Thailand.

Jo Teo is a physicist and science communicator with a master’s degree in Mathematical and Theoretical Physics from the University of Oxford. They founded Data Rakyat, a civic education initiative that breaks down current affairs through data-driven storytelling. With professional experience in education and a growing presence in civic and political advocacy, Jo leverages public platforms to make discourse in Singapore more inclusive, transparent, and evidence-based. As a Schwarzman Scholar, they aim to deepen their understanding of governance and develop scalable tools for participatory democracy that close the gap between everyday citizens and policymakers. Jo is from Singapore.

Jonathan (Jon) Eaton is a Pre-Law Fellow at Steptoe LLP, specializing in export controls, sanctions, and national security regulations. He graduated summa cum laude and Phi Beta Kappa from Colgate University, where he served as president of the Student Government Association and completed a high honors thesis, translating a 162-page Chinese comic into English. An aspiring attorney, Jon hopes to gain a deeper understanding of China’s geoeconomic security policies and their impact on private actors. He hopes to eventually apply these insights as a policymaker, bringing clarity and an international perspective to an increasingly complex regulatory landscape. Jon is from the United States.

Jordan Van Doren studied Global Affairs at Yale University, where she directed an eight-member capstone team that briefed senior leaders at U.S. Indo-Pacific Command on Sino-U.S. economic policy. She has led pro bono legal aid work supporting domestic violence survivors and mentors Ukrainian refugees through the university admissions process. At the Reischauer Center for East Asian Studies and the National Committee on U.S.–China Relations, she is developing educational initiatives on Sino-Caribbean development finance. Jordan aspires to build a career bridging Caribbean infrastructure development with China’s expanding role in foreign lending. Jordan holds citizenship from the United States, Jamaica, and Switzerland.

José Ginocchio Moraiz is an anthropologist, filmmaker, and co-founder of Cascada, an NGO focusing on water sovereignty for communities impacted by climate change. After graduating from Trinity College in Hartford, José undertook a regional-level advocacy position at an international human rights organization. His latest assignment was as field investigator for an international lawsuit about environmental justice. He has drafted and led projects of international cooperation with institutions including the European Commission and conducted fact-finding missions in nine countries. José aims to develop a Sino-Latin American model of sustainability to ensure an equitable socio-environmental transition. José is from Argentina.

Josué Godeme is a senior at Dartmouth College studying Computer Science with a minor in Chinese. He built the first dataset for the Adja language, his native tongue, and is developing the first AI translator to enable education in local African languages. As co-president of the National Society of Black Engineers and CoderDojo Dartmouth, he teaches computer science to underserved communities. Josué ranked first on Benin’s national exit exam and is a King Scholar and Stamps Scholar. He aspires to use AI and technology to make quality education accessible across Africa, particularly through native language tools. Josué is from Benin.

Joud Khateb is a Saudi investment professional at the Supreme National Investment Committee, working at the intersection of public policy and capital markets. He contributed to Saudi Arabia’s G20 Trade and Investment agenda and Expo-related international engagement. Previously at Kearney and Oliver Wyman, he delivered cross-sector results from airport journey redesigns to banking operations improvements. He holds a bachelor’s degree in Industrial and Systems Engineering from King Abdul Aziz University and has been recognized at the Geneva International Exhibition of Inventions. Joud is also a member of the Saudi Leadership Society as a graduate of the Misk Fellowship and the Ignited Voices program under the Crown Prince Mohammed Bin Salman Foundation​. Joud aims to build durable Saudi-China platforms that translate policy into pipelines for SMEs and technology transfer. Joud is from Saudi Arabia.

Ka Kiu Lau is a Hong Kong football representative team player and former captain of Pomona College’s varsity team. He has represented Hong Kong since the age of 13, competing in the 19th Asian Games and scoring his first senior goal at the HK–Guangdong Cup. He recently signed professionally with Lee Man FC for the 2025-26 season and is representing Hong Kong in qualification for the 2027 AFC Asian Cup. Off the field, Ka Kiu led cross-border strategy research at Qualcomm and analyzed healthcare investments at VMS Investment Group, deepening his interest in U.S.–China economic relations. Ka Kiu is from Hong Kong S.A.R.

Kaitlyn Tran is a senior at Harvard University studying Government and History, where she focuses on comparative politics, colonial history, and migration. These interests are reflected professionally in previous internships at the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation, Norwegian Refugee Council USA, and Bureau of East Asian and Pacific Affairs (VSFS). Kaitlyn is also passionate about international dialogues, having served as a delegate at the 69th UN Commission on the Status of Women. As a Schwarzman Scholar, she seeks to shape humanitarian and development financing in the Global South amid great power competition. Kaitlyn is from the United States.

Kalena Blake is a researcher at the Center for a New American Security, focusing on defense. Previously, she was a Strategy Analyst in Deloitte's Government and Public Services practice. She graduated from Princeton University, where she studied International Affairs, captained the Women’s Lightweight Rowing Team to four national championships, and was a managing editor of The Daily Princetonian. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Kalena hopes to advance U.S. foreign policy and navigate the U.S.–China rivalry without conflict. She grew up in Southeast Asia, speaks French and Bulgarian, and holds dual U.S. and Bulgarian citizenship.

Kendall Carll is a senior at Harvard College studying History, Government, and Chinese. Dedicated to understanding and progressively addressing the challenges of the Sino-American relationship, he has taught in Beijing, studied in Taipei, and worked in Washington, D.C. His research on U.S.–China relations and cross-strait issues has been published in leading journals, and his professional experiences span the U.S. government, academia, and various American think tanks. Kendall hopes to bridge scholarship and practice in pursuit of a more stable and constructive future for the world’s most consequential relationship. Kendall is from the United States.

Qiqi (Kiara) Shan is an undergraduate student at Cornell University, double majoring in Psychology and Economics. She is passionate about promoting positive changes in China’s mental health system through business innovation and public leadership. Kiara brings hands-on experience across healthcare, research, and community engagement, including patient experience and customer insight research at a $5B+ healthcare organization. She also led large-scale cultural initiatives as a leader of Cornell Chinese Students and Scholars Association. Motivated by the systemic challenges in mental health care, Kiara seeks to leverage her interdisciplinary background in Psychology, Economics, and Management to promote cross-sector collaboration and explore sustainable models for improving mental health accessibility. Kiara is from China.

Kilhah St Fort studies Data, Technology, & Society at CUNY Lehman College. She is the co-founder of Students Uplifting Ancestral Spaces, a student organization committed to advocating for the visibility of the contributions of enslaved Africans to the history and development of the Bronx. At Lehman College, she works as a student advisor, leads TEDxCUNY’s partnerships and outreach operations, and serves as the vice president for the Francophone Club. Kilhah aspires to develop more equitable data policies that incorporate the needs of underrepresented communities into technology design and governance. Kilhah is from the United States.

Qianwei (Kingwell) Ma studies International Politics at Georgetown University, drawing on experience across China, the U.S., and Qatar. His research on global sovereign debt, including awarded work on China’s African investments, has been presented at Harvard University and at the MPSA Annual Conference. He has gained experience in private sector roles and in public financial institutions. As a host and writer, he reconstructs narratives for Global South communities, interning for an award-winning show with five million views. As a Schwarzman Scholar, he aims to foster inclusive global financial frameworks by bridging understanding of China’s approach. Kingwell is from China.

Kwabena Owusu Sarfo recently earned his master’s degree in Energy and Resources from the University of California, Berkeley. His research explored the electricity and climate impacts of energy-intensive facilities such as data centers. He previously worked at Genser Energy in Ghana on large-scale gas and solar-hybrid projects and founded Envision Digital, a digital signage company expanding into clean energy ventures. His projects span solar desalination in Kenya, energy access in Ghana, and commercialization of methane pyrolysis through Berkeley’s Cleantech-to-Market program. He aims to connect African, American, and Chinese innovations in sustainable infrastructure, advancing equitable energy transitions and global climate. Kwabena is from Ghana.

YangFangZheng (Kyle) Li is a designer and engineer dedicated to integrating design, technology, and human experience. After graduating from Imperial College London, Kyle’s design works received Red Dot Design Awards, and his wearable product research was published at the ACM Conference on Tangible and Embedded Interaction. He previously worked at Apple as a manufacturing design engineer, contributing to the development of the iPhone and Apple Watch. Kyle is passionate about fostering global interdisciplinary collaboration and advancing design approaches that make technology more inclusive, accessible, and responsive to human needs, shaping a more innovative and human-centered future. Kyle is from China.

Ronan (Laith) Aljohmani graduated in Dentistry from Trinity College Dublin, where he was elected a Foundation Scholar in 2021, the country’s highest undergraduate academic honor. He earned the Costello Medal for national public health research and has delivered emergency dental care to refugee populations, including on a humanitarian mission in Syria. A diplomat and member of the Royal College of Surgeons Ireland, he completed a fully funded master’s degree in Tissue Engineering at RCSI and received a scholarship in medicine from Trinity College Dublin. Laith aspires to lead healthcare innovation and biotechnology, forging global partnerships — particularly with China — to drive scalable impact in his home communities. Laith is from Ireland.

Laxman Bist is the founder and CEO of the Institute for Rural Development, an NGO that empowers youths to solve the leadership crisis and foster sustainable development in Nepal through its rapidly growing global network of Nepalis. It has over 50 chapters across universities and the Nepali diaspora globally. He has led over 20 projects across diverse developmental sectors and has collaborated with several governmental bodies, including the Prime Minister's Office of Nepal. Laxman graduated from Dartmouth College, where he studied physics and neuroscience. During his time as a Schwarzman Scholar, he hopes to diversify his leadership and entrepreneurial skills. Laxman is from Nepal.

Leonardo Alves Amaral Torres is a tech entrepreneur advancing public safety in Brazil, where his product leadership has helped law enforcement solve over 10,000 crimes. As product manager for an app used by 75,000 citizens to share crime-scene video evidence, he leads a multidisciplinary team building scalable technology for safer cities. He holds a bachelor’s degree in Electrical Engineering from the Universidade de São Paulo and earned a merit-based scholarship for his master’s degree in France. At COP27, he represented his French university as a youth delegate. Through Schwarzman Scholars, he will study China’s green finance models to launch a Brazilian sustainability investment fund. Leonardo is from Brazil.

Leonas Pausch graduated with first-class honors with distinction, at the top of his cohort from the University of Cambridge. He's also studied Business Administration and Economics in Germany. At Cambridge, he served as vice president and trustee of the Cambridge Union and further led multiple political and cultural societies. After graduation, he worked at a geopolitical think tank in London and a major German bank in New York, which both link to his long-term aspiration to make financial globalization more stable, fair, and resilient. Leonas hopes to use his time in China to understand Beijing’s initiatives to reshape and challenge existing institutions. Leonas is from Germany.

Lisa Shen is a clinical scientist and managing director at The Counsly Group, where she expands college and career access for communities facing socioeconomic barriers and substance use challenges. She earned her B.A. in Psychology and Political Science from Swarthmore College and later directed operations for a $5.5 million NIH study at the University of Rhode Island, developing mobile health interventions for individuals with PTSD and opioid use disorder. Through Schwarzman Scholars, she will study how China’s “triple helix” model of innovation accelerates the translation of research into scalable digital mental health therapeutics. Lisa is from the United States.

Lucas San Miguel is passionate about economic development and aspires to work in U.S. diplomacy on international public-private sector partnerships impacting Chinese and Latin American markets. He’s worked for The Coca-Cola Company for over four years on integrated, Gen Z marketing projects in North America, Asia, Europe, and South America, gaining experience localizing global business strategies to nuanced cultures. He led Gen Z marketing strategy for Costa Coffee’s U.S. expansion. Lucas also consulted with KPMG on technology transformations, and he hopes to further explore China’s leadership in technology-driven development to advance multilateral support for international cooperation. Lucas is from the United States.

Maha AlAbduljabbar is a consultant at BCG and a social entrepreneur who is passionate about innovation and global diplomacy. As the curator of the WEF Riyadh Global Shapers, she advanced over 50 initiatives, earning the #1 hub project ranking in MENA. She founded SPARK Social Innovation and Tura, an NGO backed by HRH the Crown Prince’s Misk Foundation. Recognized as a Young Arab Pioneer, a two-time S4CC awardee, and a recipient of BCG’s global Key to Purpose, Maha holds two Guinness World Records for social impact projects, participating on over 40 platforms from the G20 Y20 to UN ECOSOC to drive inclusive development. Maha is from Saudi Arabia.

Max Palys is a senior at Harvard College studying Mathematics and East Asian Studies. He serves as the editorial chair of The Harvard Crimson, where he promotes the values of respectful disagreement and has edited op-eds by prominent professors and politicians. Within mathematics, Max studies dynamical systems and bifurcation theory with an eye toward applications. Fluent in Mandarin and trained in Classical Chinese, he aims to build bilingual, evidence-based communication between China and the United States. Max is from the United States and Lithuania.

Maya Prakash is a senior at Williams College studying Economics and Comparative Literature. An inaugural Global Scholar, she also spent a year at Oxford through the Williams-Exeter Programme. At Williams, she led the Williams Record’s opinions section and has interned at The Washington Post. As a Schwarzman Scholar, she hopes to deepen her understanding of the U.S.-India-China dynamic and foster more informed conversations on China through opinion journalism and public discourse. She is an Indian-American raised in Singapore and Hong Kong.

Mehakpreet (Mehak) Saggu is transforming how the world learns research and neuroscience. As a Pearson Scholar at the University of Toronto, she has reached over 700 students across 30 countries and 120 institutions. Her programs aim to bring neuroscience literacy to 500,000 rural students by 2030. Blending curiosity with action, she has co-authored seven manuscripts, completed 1,200 hours of clinical shadowing in neurology and oncology, and helped raise $912,000 for global causes. She believes research truly comes alive only when it reaches the people whose lives it seeks to transform. Mehak is from India.

Mira Jiang studies computational neuroscience and English literature at Harvard.
