She serves as president of the Creative Writing Collective, the largest student writing organization on campus, co-chairs the Leverett House Committee, and was the editor-in-chief of Prescriptions, the undergraduate medical humanities publication. A Young Guts Scholar of the American Gastroenterological Association, Mira researches how microbes and hormones shape the enteric nervous system at Boston Children’s Hospital and has worked at the RIKEN Center for Brain Science in Japan, building neural networks to model Alzheimer’s disease. She hopes to shape the integration of AI into medicine, bridging scientific innovation with cultural traditions. Mira is from the United States.

Mohammed Mahdi Obaid is a leader in global anti-corruption efforts who has gone from starting an organization that has trained over 300 Iraqis in leadership and rule of law to suing pharmaceutical companies in U.S. courts for corrupt business practices in Iraq. Highly engaged with government and diplomatic circles, he has organized the Kurdish government's first international advocacy forum, conducted assessments on 2019 Iraqi protests for foreign diplomatic missions, and managed $100,000 in grant funds from the U.S. Department of State. He is an aspiring international lawyer dedicated to supporting Iraq’s institutional and economic development. He is Kurdish and from Iraq.

Oluwamayowa (Naomi) Adeniji graduated with a degree in Civil, Structural, and Environmental Engineering from Trinity College Dublin. With roots in both Ireland and Nigeria, they are passionate about advancing sustainability. Naomi combines an analytical approach with collaborative leadership in their aspirations to address complex challenges in the energy transition. Their work spans climate-focused innovation projects and inclusive initiatives such as founding Trinity’s Equality in STEM Society and mentoring at Teen-Turn. With a global outlook and a particular interest in China’s role in shaping sustainable technologies, Naomi aspires to drive systemic change at the intersection of environment, equity, and innovation. Naomi is from Ireland and Nigeria.

Natalie Delille is a Haitian-American social impact leader. She founded DearSociety, a nonprofit that has provided 150 scholarships to underserved youth in Haiti and broader educational opportunities to disadvantaged communities to break cycles of poverty. She studied business at Howard University, earned a master’s in AI Ethics & Society from the University of Cambridge, and completed a master’s in Policy at Georgetown University. Natalie spent six years at Visa, where she led financial inclusion programs and inclusive workplace initiatives impacting over 25,000 employees. She aspires to collaboratively transform Haiti's development and has an interest in China's poverty alleviation strategies. Natalie is from the United States.

Noah Tan is a Gaither Junior Fellow at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, specializing in technology and international security. He earned a bachelor’s degree in International Relations with honors and distinction from Stanford University, where his thesis focused on U.S.–China trade and tariff policy. At Stanford, he was a research affiliate at the Center for International Security and Cooperation, a student fellow at the Hoover Institution, a coxswain for the Men’s Rowing Team, the president of the Pre-Law Society, and the editor-in-chief of the Journal of International Affairs. He will soon pursue a J.D. at Harvard Law School. Noah is from the United States.

Nomboh Evans Kunchu is a Mastercard Foundation Scholar and an Electrical Power Systems Engineering graduate from African Leadership University in Mauritius. He is the founder of Moto Health, a startup providing mobility solutions for minor health emergencies in underserved African communities, which has raised more than $25,000 in grant funding. Evans is an alumnus of the ALU Global Leadership Program in Silicon Valley and helped lead ALC Valley, which raised $8,500 to support young African entrepreneurs. After Schwarzman Scholars, he plans to scale Moto Health by applying lessons from China’s health and innovation systems. Nomboh is from Cameroon.

Parker Ritzmann is a President Scholar at the University of Washington, where he is pursuing a Bachelor of Arts in Business Administration. Passionate about finance and startups, he is part of the Lavin Entrepreneurship Program and interned at Bain & Company and Two Ravens Venture Capital. Trained in Florence as a certified barista, Parker founded HOPE Neighborhood Roasters, a social enterprise empowering foster youth through specialty coffee. As a Schwarzman Scholar, he aims to transfer insights from China’s development trajectory to create economic opportunity and equitable value capture along supply chains in the Global South. Parker is from the United States.

Patricio Ortiz will graduate from Stanford University with a B.A. in Economics and M.S. in Statistics, where he restarted an international U.S.–China student summit and serves as a voting member on Stanford's Board of Trustees. He has interned at the World Bank and the Asian Development Bank, where he spearheaded the shift of the $180 billion loan portfolio to sovereigns. He has also interned at private equity and hedge funds based in Singapore and Japan, affecting emerging markets across Asia. He aims to bridge public and private capital markets to unlock financing for emerging economies. Patricio is from Bolivia and the United States.

Paw Mar Gay is dedicated to improving global child health policies and increasing access to education. Paw Mar graduated from Stanford University with a Bachelor of Science in Human Biology. At Stanford, she served as an Education Fellow with the Stanford Haas Center for Public Service and earned the Award of Excellence and the Community Impact Award for her work in promoting literacy and mental well-being in the San Francisco Bay Area. She has also hosted conferences promoting first-generation and/or low-income identity in higher education for college students, administrators, and educators across the United States as Conference Chair. Paw Mar is from the United States.

Peter Scheuer is an undergraduate student at Tsinghua University, focusing on economics and sports management. Peter aims to build a global sports platform that connects training, competition, and cultural exchange. He has competed in International Table Tennis Federation and World Table Tennis events, earning the title of U.S. Nationals under-21 quarter-finalist in 2023. As an influencer with over 165,000 followers on social media, Peter promotes the idea that sport can bridge cultural and political divides. He captains Tsinghua University Table Tennis Team, winning multiple men's singles and team titles. Peter is from the United States.

Pranav Pattatathunaduvil is a second-year MPP student in the Yale Jackson School’s BA/MPP Program in Global Affairs. He is passionate about Indo-Pacific geopolitics, especially U.S.–India–China relations and the region’s tech competition, and aspires to work in foreign policy. Pranav is the co-founder of the GeoTech Initiative, which aims to get students involved at the intersection of tech and international relations, and he spearheaded the first and second GeoTech Forums, which convened over 200 STEM and policy students from around the U.S. in Washington, D.C. He has previously interned at the State Department and U.S. Indo-Pacific Command. Pranav is from the United States.

Priyanka Mukhara is a senior at Harvard College studying Social Studies and Ethnicity, Migration, Rights. The first South Asian elected co-president of the Harvard-Radcliffe Asian American Association since its founding in 1976, Priyanka has united AAA's 1,000+ members and earned university recognition for championing pan-Asian diversity. Passionate about educational equity, Priyanka founded Education for Equality to train hundreds of youth advocates, co-authored legislation advancing inclusive education, and secured $3.7 million in grants expanding Ethnic Studies curricula nationwide. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Priyanka seeks to forge a global vision for education policy rooted in equity. Priyanka is from the United States.

Rachel Ou is a researcher, artist, and community leader passionate about equitable healthcare. At the University of Pennsylvania, she has researched nanoparticle drug delivery and autoimmune disease, and guided uninsured patients through surgery access at the Center for Surgical Health. A member of the 2025 World University Games table tennis team, Rachel builds bridges across sport and culture. As president of Penn Chinese Calligraphy, she fosters cross-cultural dialogue through art. She founded ArtInMind, a nonprofit using art to empower children with disabilities, and aspires to be a physician advancing US-China biotechnology and healthcare collaboration. Rachel is from the United States.

Ruihao (Ray) Lin graduated from Tsinghua University and Cornell University, where he served as an editor of the Cornell Law Review and the Cornell Legal Information Institute. He has contributed to policy research at the Asian Development Bank and provided pro bono legal support for Afghan nationals navigating resettlement processes. His academic interests center on global justice, comparative legal systems, and cross-border legal collaboration, with a commitment to strengthening international cooperation. Ray is from China.

Richard Wilson II is a deal analyst at Harlem Capital, where he supports sourcing and due diligence for high-impact investment opportunities. Previously a technical program manager at Meta, he worked across supply chain, product refinement, and quality measurement functions throughout his career. Richard has completed venture fellowships at Keyhorse Capital, Ripple Ventures, and YC-backed startups. He earned his B.S. and M.Eng. in Industrial Engineering and Engineering Management, respectively, from the University of Louisville. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Richard hopes to understand China’s venture capital ecosystem to help diverse-led VC firms ensure financial sustainability. Richard is from the United States.

Rishika Kartik is a senior at Brown University studying Biology and Accessible Design, a degree she pioneered. A U.S. Presidential Scholar and TEDx speaker with millions of views, she advances disability and assistive technology research while expanding artistic opportunities for blind communities through Touch and Create Studios, which she founded. She developed a healthy aging program at Hawaii’s leading Center for the Blind and has spoken at institutions, including Johns Hopkins University and Johnson & Johnson. Rishika integrates science and design to support global conversations on disability, technology, and inclusive design. Rishika is from the United States.

Rui Huang earned a bachelor's degree in Economics and Cognitive Studies from Vanderbilt University. As president of the Chinese Students and Scholars Association (VUCSSA), Rui launched mental wellness initiatives and partnered with Vanderbilt’s Student Care Network to support international students. Rui also hosts a mental health podcast guiding global listeners through identity-based struggles. As a mindfulness coach and research assistant, Rui developed evidence-based interventions for youth mental health. Rui will pursue a master’s in Mental Health Counseling at Columbia University, committed to advancing culturally inclusive emotional resilience and promoting global mental health awareness across borders. Rui is from China.

Ryan Alezz is a researcher and community leader passionate about strengthening healthcare systems in resource-limited settings. At Johns Hopkins University, he served as president of the Muslim Student Association, where he expanded interfaith partnerships and secured lasting institutional support for religious accommodations. He led engineering teams to design a pacemaker training simulator and an automated food distribution system for food deserts. His global health research includes evaluating oxygen infrastructure in West Africa and mental health programs for refugees on the Thai–Myanmar border. Ryan aspires to be a physician-engineer advancing health equity worldwide. Ryan is a citizen of the United States.

Xueran (Sabrina) Ma will gradute with degrees in Philosophy and FinTech from Renmin University of China, where she is an ice hockey player and the recipient of the Entrepreneurship Star Award. She founded a healthtech startup supporting Alzheimer’s patients through digital screening tools, and has organised community campaigns in ageing neighbourhoods to raise awareness of chronic disease prevention. Through internships at KKR, NDRC, China Securities, and BCG, she has deepened her interest in public policy and investment. Xueran aims to globalise China’s health technology sector and foster cross-border collaboration. Xueran is from China.

Sabrina Steinberga is an analyst at Barclays Corporate & Investment Bank, with a joint honors degree in Business and Economics from Trinity College Dublin. She holds an active leadership role on the Young People's Committee of the National Youth Council of Ireland, advocating for education on sustainable finance. Selected for the European Leadership Academy, Sabrina has spoken at the Mobile World Congress and Ireland's Internet Governance Forum. She aspires to leverage China's innovation and expertise, designing accessible financing models that accelerate the development of sustainable infrastructure, empowering communities to lead in the energy transition. Sabrina is from Ireland and Latvia.

Samuel Levy holds a B.A. in Political Science and Global Studies from the University of Chicago and previously studied at the Kivunim Institute in Jerusalem and at National Taiwan University as a Boren Scholar. Sam hopes to explore China’s economic development model and how it has influenced its foreign policy decision making. Sam is from the United States.

Sam McKnight is the founder and director of Khthon, a global NGO pioneering the use of open-source and satellite intelligence to uncover mass graves and document war crimes. He also created KALOS, a groundbreaking initiative supporting survivors of torture in post-Assad Syria. As a digital leader, Sam has built an audience of more than 350,000 through impactful storytelling on history and humanitarian issues and was selected as one of the St. Gallen Symposium’s 200 Leaders of Tomorrow. He aims to advance accountability and innovative justice mechanisms worldwide. Sam is from the United Kingdom.

Sara Torres is the Outreach Officer at the TIDE Centre at the University of Oxford, where she leads engagement with partners on industrial policy, technology transfer, and biodiversity for innovation across the Global South. She has political risk analysis experience with leading think tanks, including the Wilson Center and the Council on Foreign Relations, and with consulting firms, including Eurasia Group. She co-founded the Oxford Latin American Society, and, earlier, the Tufts Latin American Committee. She holds a Master of Philosophy in Latin American Studies from the University of Oxford and bachelor’s degrees in International Relations and Latin American Studies from Tufts University. Sara is from Colombia and Spain.

Sarah Syed is a lawyer, trailblazer, and musician who empowers others to find their voice. She holds an Honors Bachelor of Science with distinction from the University of Toronto and a Juris Doctor from the University of Windsor. She co-founded Global Youth Impact, an NGO mobilizing thousands of youth through social justice and empowerment. Her micro-documentary on intellectual property earned recognition from the World Intellectual Property Organization. As a singer-songwriter with DEYSofficial, she writes and performs songs on global issues featured by the United Nations. Sarah aims to foster cross-cultural collaboration through China’s innovation and education. Sarah is from Canada.

Shunwen (Schuyler) Wei, a student at Tsinghua University, majors in Philosophy, Politics, and Economics. He has interned at FAO, IFAD, BCG, where he developed a passion for supporting Chinese entrepreneurs in starting their businesses in Africa. At Tsinghua, he was the president of the Tsinghua Impact Consulting Association, vice president of Tsinghua Student Entrepreneurship Association, and a key member of the Tsinghua Global Youth Dialogue, honing skills in leadership and cross-cultural exchange. He is also an enthusiast of frisbee, founding Frisbee Links, a global program where he combines his enthusiasm for frisbee with global youth engagement. Schuyler is from China.

Shankai Wei is a researcher in environmental and energy policy whose work bridges scientific analysis and global governance. As the co-author of two peer-reviewed studies on vegetation greenness under climate extremes, he has contributed to understanding climate resilience across China and globally. At UCL, he leads cross-institutional research on the marginal carbon cost curve through afforestation and previously coordinated international conferences on carbon budget governance. Shankai chose to study sustainability in addition to economics, aiming to develop equitable transition frameworks through data and diplomacy. He aspires to shape climate policy that reflects both environmental science and social justice. He is a citizen of China.

Shir Diner will receive her MD from Ben Gurion University and is committed to using medicine to advance innovation, peace, and equal opportunity. As Deputy CEO of One Heart, a humanitarian organization, she led international collaborations to strengthen healthcare systems in crisis regions worldwide and emergency responses in areas affected by war and natural disasters. She is also engaged in research aimed at promoting innovation and advancing equitable, high-quality medical care. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Shir seeks to build global networks for crisis response and explore how China’s innovation and strategies can inspire international cooperation. Shir is from Israel and the United States.

Sholpan Arzhan is a human rights professional at the United Nations, dedicated to sustaining human dignity for all. She began her international career at the International Atomic Energy Agency in Vienna before joining UN Human Rights in Geneva, where she manages UN Humanitarian Funds' projects that support more than 60,000 survivors of torture, modern slavery, and trafficking annually. The 2025 U.S. funding withdrawal became a pivotal moment, revealing the fragility of mechanisms tied to political cycles and inspiring her to explore sustainable models bridging governments, civil society, and the private sector. Sholpan is from Kazakhstan.

Sonabayim Huseynzade is a robotics researcher specializing in humanoid systems, with peer-reviewed publications and international projects across France, Belgium, Germany, and Japan. Her work focuses on emotionally intelligent interaction and ethical deployment, contributing to real-world pilots and lab prototypes. She chairs the IEEE Women in Engineering Affinity Group in Azerbaijan, where she organized initiatives, including a flagship conference panel that engaged over 700 women engineers and students. Sonabayim aims to advance culturally inclusive, trustworthy AI and human-robot interaction for care and public-interest settings. She holds a bachelor's in Mechatronics and Robotics from Azerbaijan State Oil University. Sonabayim is from Azerbaijan.

Stephanie Anderson is a graduate of New York University Shanghai, where she graduated with a degree in Political Science, minored in Mandarin, and served as student body president. She is currently working with Fulbright South Korea as an English teacher to around 600 South Korean middle school students. Stephanie is passionate about women’s inclusion in athletic spaces, having coached women’s soccer teams in Shanghai, Abu Dhabi, Paris, and now South Korea. Stephanie hopes to work with the U.S. Department of State as a foreign service officer and improve political relationships between the United States and China. Stephanie is from the United States.

Yufei (Stephanie) Li earned her undergraduate degree from Duke Kunshan University and is pursuing a Master of Public Administration with a concentration in Climate, Energy, and Environment at Columbia University. At Columbia, she served as the president of the Chinese Students and Scholars Association. Her graduate work focuses on climate governance and corporate sustainability in both domestic and international contexts. As a Schwarzman Scholar, she aims to advance sustainability through policy innovation and business solutions, strengthening China’s role in global cooperation. Stephanie is originally from China.

Su Cizem is an AI governance analyst dedicated to building international cooperation around advanced AI. She has worked with leading think tanks, including the French Center for AI Safety and The Future Society, where she helped shape the EU’s GPAI Code of Practice and convened global coalitions on frontier AI risks. As a 2025 fellow at the Institute for AI Policy and Strategy, she is developing emergency preparedness protocols for AI crises for multilateral adoption. She holds an MSc in AI and Ethics from Northeastern University London and a B.A. in Philosophy from the University of California, Los Angeles. Su is from the United States and Turkey.

Sung Jae (Harry) Park graduated with an MSc in Sociology from the University of Oxford and a BSc in Politics from the London School of Economics and Political Science, specializing in mobilization studies, particularly those that involve extreme political violence, including terrorism. This academic focus shapes his professional dedication to conflict prevention. Harry has worked with the British Red Cross, the U.K. Parliament, the government of South Korea, and the UNHCR. As a Schwarzman Scholar, he aims to examine the emerging threat of radicalization in strongly cohesive societies, taking China as a case study. He is currently fulfilling his national service as a Military Security Observer. Harry is from South Korea.

Sydney Roberts served as the student body president at the University of California, Berkeley, representing 33,000 students while earning a B.A. in Political Science and African American Studies, with minors in Public Policy and Race and the Law. As president, she secured a $195,000 grant to advance multicultural initiatives and oversaw an 800-member organization with a $1.7 million budget. Currently at EMILYs List, she helps elect Democratic, pro-choice women to political office nationwide. Sydney plans to use her leadership and political communication skills to pursue global conflict management and address shared challenges between the U.S. and China. Sydney is from the United States.

Tage Fossgard-Moser is interested in the nexus of finance, geopolitics, and climate change and is aiming to contribute to connecting the East and the West in the critical mineral industry. He is currently completing his BSc in International Business and Chinese at Copenhagen Business School. Outside of his studies, he has previously led multiple initiatives, including a large research project between Western academics and a Chinese mineral company, as well as internships at Boston Consulting Group and at various investment banks. Tage is from Norway and the United Kingdom.

Tiffanie Qing Yi Laborie-Bousquet works at the nexus of sustainable finance, philanthropy, and multilateral policy. As a fellow at Oak Foundation, she supports a $35 million portfolio for global climate initiatives, with a focus on renewable energy in China. She also works as a partnerships assistant with the UN World Food Program in Geneva. Tiffanie received the Davis Projects for Peace scholarship to develop a community-led mobile application for Maasai pastoralists in Kenya, using solar-powered tools to reduce climate-induced conflicts. As a Schwarzman Scholar, she aims to strengthen China–Global South partnerships in climate finance and renewable energy. Tiffanie is from France.

Tiansheng (Travis) Ye, a global business and finance student at The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, is the founder of a biotech startup developing sustainable, insect-derived biofuel solutions. As an impact-driven Hong Kong Jockey Club Scholar, he has launched a water-saving program during the water crisis in Cape Town, led empowerment programs for students from rural Hunan, and founded a campus internationalization initiative engaging 2,000 students in Hong Kong. With experience living in five countries and a problem-solving mindset honed during his time at McKinsey, he aspires to drive global partnerships in sustainable innovation. Travis is from China.

Truc Cam (Cam) Nguyen is a leader dedicated to combating human trafficking. A Stanford University alumna of Symbolic Systems and Human Rights, she previously founded an award-winning Vietnamese nonprofit supporting over 10,000 community members and victims. At Stanford, she served as a Confidential Support Team student representative, an editor at Intersect, and conducted research with the Virtual Human Interaction Lab and Hoover Institution. A 2023 TreeHacks Grand Prize winner with experience in virtual reality startups, Cam aims to leverage her background in emerging technologies to develop global anti-trafficking solutions through Schwarzman Scholars alongside a future career in law. Cam is from Vietnam.

Tulisha Malichi is a Mastercard Foundation Scholar at EARTH University and the founder of SOLGANIC, a startup addressing the challenge of soil degradation. An advocate for food systems, he has taken on leadership roles as an ambassador for the Next Generation Agriculture Impact Network and has spoken at major forums, including to senior business leaders during a food systems executive leadership program. He is currently an intern at the UNDP and previously completed a research internship at Cornell University. Tulisha hopes to shape agricultural policies that transform food systems and support sustainable rural development. Tulisha is from Zambia.

Weijing (Vickie) Liu is a learning designer at the Tsinghua University Lab for Lifelong Learning, where she develops innovative learning experiences that make education more playful and inclusive. Having navigated the Chinese, U.K., and U.S. educational systems, she brings interdisciplinary and cross-cultural perspectives to her work. She has been a research assistant with the MIT Media Lab and designed learning games at Microsoft Research Asia, focusing on human-AI interaction and educational innovation. She holds a bachelor’s degree in Psychology from Mount Holyoke College and a master’s degree in Learning Design, Innovation, and Technology from Harvard University. Vickie is from China.

Francis (Xavier) Ramirez is advancing universities across Indiana to ensure they remain cornerstones of ingenuity worldwide. He is the founding staff member in Indiana University's Office of the Chief Operating Officer, supporting policy coordination across a 45,000-employee institution. He also serves as Director of Recruitment for the Orr Fellowship, a nonprofit developing the next generation of business leaders. There, he heads a 120-person team, drawing talent from over 16 universities across the United States. As a Schwarzman Scholar, Xavier will explore how China's higher education models balance national priorities with long-term stability. Xavier is from the United States.

Xuanlin Tang is a graduate of Tsinghua University, where she won the university’s Challenge Cup championship for her research. She twice earned China’s National Scholarship, was recognized as an academic top student under the Ministry of Education’s 101 Program, and represented the 3rd Tsinghua Youth Dialogue. She also captained the Tsinghua Rixin College running team, founded the university’s first English speech association, and led English public speaking workshops. During her exchange at Cornell University, she joined the Undergraduate Research Board, served as a peer mentor, and competed in U.S. collegiate speech tournaments. Xuanlin is from China.

Xueyi Zhou is a humanitarian worker with field experiences in Somalia, Bangladesh, and Moldova. In Somalia, she led proposal development and project management worth over $35 million, which delivered multisectoral support to over 200,000 internally displaced Somalis. Before the humanitarian career, she graduated from Sciences Po in Paris with a master’s degree in International Development and dual bachelor's degrees in International Politics and Translation and Interpreting at Zhejiang University, where she was one of the 10th cohort Morningside Cultural China Scholars. Through Schwarzman Scholars, she aims to facilitate China’s engagement in humanitarian assistance systems and China’s leadership in driving transformative change. Xueyi is from China.

Yash Chudasama is a former consultant at PwC and a deferred MBA admit at Stanford Graduate School of Business. Passionate about entrepreneurship, he co-founded the ALC Valley, his university’s first student-led entrepreneurship hub, supporting over 30 startups. He currently works as an operator at Gaya, a U.S.-based tech startup using AI to automate data entry and streamline insurance quoting workflows. Yash leads strategic initiatives, drives operational efficiency, and is also co-leading the startup’s Series-A funding round. Through Schwarzman Scholars, Yash aims to explore China’s innovation ecosystem and leadership model to broaden his global entrepreneurial perspective. Yash is from Tanzania.

Yijia Cao graduated from New York University Shanghai with a degree in Computer Science and minors in Mathematics and Cybersecurity. She is passionate about applying AI, cybersecurity, and quantitative finance to create broader social impact, conducting research projects at Carnegie Mellon University and NYU, and applying mathematical and data-driven approaches to financial research in both academic and professional settings. As part of an engineering team partnering with the Changzhou government, she contributes to smart city development across sectors such as urban management and emergency response, improving the lives of millions. An award-winning composer, she expresses her cultural identity through music for international audiences. Yijia is from China.

Yixi (Joe) Zhang will graduate from the University of Chicago with a bachelor’s degree in Economics. His research examines international security and Chinese foreign policy, focusing on the intersection of law and global governance. He has won awards at numerous international law tournaments and, as coach of the UChicago Moot Court team, has led student advocates in arbitration competitions while organizing events that foster inter-community dialogue. He has also interned at various legal institutions and the World Peace Forum. Beyond academics, he supports programs that empower local youth through debate and communication training. Joe is from China.

Yixuan Guo is dedicated to advancing gender education and women’s empowerment in China. As an applied mathematics student and founder of a multicultural community space, she brings together diverse voices to promote overlooked female role models and inspire collective dialogue. She believes that true empowerment unites mental awareness and physical strength, helping women grow holistically. In the future, she plans to engage in corporate management and advance a sustainable, nationwide initiative for gender education. Yixuan is from China.

Yixuan Zhang graduated from Beijing Foreign Studies University with a degree in Portuguese. She is dedicated to promoting tech-driven impact investment in emerging markets (EMs), like Latin America and Small Island Developing States (SIDS), to catalyze a more efficient, inclusive, and equitable global economy. Her professional endeavors span the public sector, private equity, venture capital firms, and tech startups, where she has contributed to China Aid initiatives, early-stage overseas investments across EMs, and large-scale M&A transactions, spearheading rigorous valuation and strategic due diligence of target companies. As an investment influencer, Yixuan has demonstrated foresight in identifying opportunities that inspired independent LPs to invest in Chinese tech ventures. Yixuan is from China.

Yujing Zhang is a global citizen fluent in Arabic, Chinese, French, and English, with a passion for cross-cultural economic development. She holds a master’s degree in East Asian Studies from Stanford University and graduated summa cum laude from New York University with a double major in Global Liberal Studies and French and a minor in Middle Eastern & Islamic Studies. As a fixed income credit analyst at China Merchants Bank International in Hong Kong, she authored daily analysis reports on Asia-Pacific and European corporate bonds to guide investment decisions. She previously interned as a marketing strategist in Accra, Ghana, supporting women’s empowerment initiatives. Yujing is from Guangzhou, China.

Zarrish Ahmed studied at Georgetown University in Qatar, majoring in International Politics with a certificate in South Asian Studies. She has spearheaded flagship programs like Hoya Welcome Week and First Year Experience, building inclusive communities for over 200 peers. She also served as student government vice president and chief of staff, where she oversaw governance of over 500 students. Zarrish has interned at Pakistan’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs and worked at the Center for Security and Emerging Technologies, analyzing Chinese tech developments. Her research on flood governance has been presented internationally. Through community-focused policymaking, Zarrish aspires to bridge state-society divides in Pakistan. Zarrish is from Pakistan.
